_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Fayette County, PA. Formed 1783 from Westmoreland. County seat at Uniontown. Epicenter of the Whiskey Rebellion. Braddock's Road corridor. National Road brought prosperity. Record survival is good, with courthouse records running from county formation.

previous_courthouses.fate: First courthouse — a log structure typical of frontier county seats. Served during the Whiskey Rebellion era. Replaced as the county grew.
Stone courthouse. Uniontown was a National Road town and the courthouse served a growing population of travelers, merchants, and farmers.
Brick courthouse. Served through the Civil War and early coal/coke era. Replaced by the current Romanesque structure in 1891.

record_losses.cause: None — no major fire or catastrophic loss at the Uniontown courthouse
record_losses.description: Fayette County's courthouse has not experienced a catastrophic fire or natural disaster. Records run continuously from the county's 1783 formation. Early loose papers show some deterioration, but bound volumes are intact. The Whiskey Rebellion (1794) did not damage courthouse records — the insurgency's targets were excise officers and their property, not the county court.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Fayette County after 1783, the records are there. The Whiskey Rebellion left political scars but no record gaps. Fayette County's continuous deed series from 1783 is one of the best in western PA.
record_losses.source: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.

deeds.gaps: No known significant gaps. Pre-1783 Westmoreland County deed books cover the Fayette area before separation.
deeds.repository: Fayette County Recorder of Deeds, Uniontown; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com PA land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — will books from near-formation. Orphans' Court records provide additional estate settlement detail.
wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose estate papers are fragmentary.
wills_estates.repository: Fayette County Register of Wills; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

marriages.completeness: Marriage licenses required in Pennsylvania from 1885. Pre-1885 marriages documented through church records and published notices.
marriages.gaps: The 102-year gap between county formation (1783) and marriage license law (1885) requires church and newspaper research.
marriages.repository: Fayette County Clerk of the Orphans' Court; Pennsylvania State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good — continuous from formation. Quarter sessions records cover criminal, civil, and administrative business.
court_minutes.gaps: No significant gaps in bound volumes.
court_minutes.repository: Fayette County Prothonotary; Pennsylvania State Archives microfilm

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1906. Coal and coke company towns had particularly uneven compliance.
vital_records.repository: Pennsylvania Department of Health; Pennsylvania State Archives

substitute_sources.source_type: Whiskey Rebellion records — federal and state
substitute_sources.description: The Whiskey Rebellion of 1794 generated extensive federal records: court proceedings, depositions, petitions for pardon, military records of the expedition. These documents name hundreds of Fayette County residents and provide detail about land ownership, occupation, political allegiance, and neighborhood relationships.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (federal court records); Pennsylvania State Archives; Fayette County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Extremely valuable for identifying late-eighteenth-century residents. Deposition records provide neighbor and family relationships. Pardon petitions detail personal circumstances. Military muster rolls for the federal expedition identify participants from both sides.
substitute_sources.source: Slaughter, Thomas P. The Whiskey Rebellion: Frontier Epilogue to the American Revolution. New York: Oxford University Press, 1986.

substitute_sources.description: The H.C. Frick Coke Company dominated Fayette County's Connellsville coke region from the 1870s through U.S. Steel's acquisition in 1901. Company records include payroll ledgers, housing records, company store ledgers, and injury reports. The Connellsville coke region employed thousands of immigrant workers.
substitute_sources.repository: Frick Collection Archives; Hagley Museum and Library, Wilmington, DE; University of Pittsburgh Archives of Industrial Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for immigrant worker families. Payroll records document names, wages, and dates of employment. Housing records place families at specific company town addresses. Injury and death reports provide detail not found in county records.
substitute_sources.source: Warren, Kenneth. Triumphant Capitalism: Henry Clay Frick and the Industrial Transformation of America. Pittsburgh: University of Pittsburgh Press, 1996.

substitute_sources.source_type: Federal census schedules and immigration records
substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940 with supplemental agricultural and manufacturing schedules. Immigration-era Fayette County census records are multilingual — enumerators often phonetically approximated Eastern European names.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential. Manufacturing schedules (1850–1880) document early iron furnaces and distilleries. The 1900 and 1910 censuses capture the immigration wave and list country of origin, year of immigration, and language spoken.

narrative_hooks: Fayette County's records run unbroken from 1783 — and if {ancestor_name} was anywhere near the Whiskey Rebellion, the federal depositions and pardon petitions name hundreds of participants and witnesses who would otherwise be invisible in the county records.

Pennsylvania did not require marriage licenses until 1885 — a century after Fayette County was formed. If {ancestor_name} married in Fayette County before that date, the church records are essential, particularly the Presbyterian session minutes that documented frontier marriages.

If {ancestor_name} worked in the Connellsville coke region, the Frick Company records may hold more about {pronoun_object} than the county courthouse — payroll, housing assignments, and injury reports that documented immigrant life in ways the county recorder never attempted.

The census enumerator who recorded {ancestor_name}'s name in the Connellsville coke fields likely spelled it phonetically from a language {pronoun_subject} did not speak — the name in the record may bear little resemblance to the name the family used.
source_references: Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission. Guide to the Record Groups in the Pennsylvania State Archives.
Slaughter, Thomas P. The Whiskey Rebellion: Frontier Epilogue to the American Revolution. New York: Oxford University Press, 1986.
Warren, Kenneth. Triumphant Capitalism: Henry Clay Frick and the Industrial Transformation of America. Pittsburgh: University of Pittsburgh Press, 1996.
Fayette County Historical Society. Collections and Finding Aids.
